headline: Marias Massacre Claims Piegan Blackfeet Lives, 1870
body: The winter of 1870 held the land in a cruel grip, its icy breath a harbinger of deeper cold. Along the Marias River, in what was then Montana Territory, a peaceful encampment of Piegan Blackfeet huddled against the biting wind. They were led by Chief Heavy Runner, a man who carried a paper, a promise of safety from the Indian Bureau, a shield against the encroaching tide of soldiers. But promises, like breath in the frigid air, could vanish. The United States Army, under the command of Major Eugene M. Baker, moved with a grim purpose. Their target, they believed, was Mountain Chief's hostile band, responsible for recent depredations. Yet, in the predawn hours of January 23, 1870, a terrible error was made. Baker's command, a mix of cavalry and infantry, found not Mountain Chief's warriors, but Heavy Runner's people, many weakened by smallpox, most of their healthy men away hunting. The camp, a collection of thirty-two lodges, held primarily women, children, and the elderly. It was a scene of vulnerability, not aggression. Scout Joe Kipp, familiar with the bands, recognized the peaceful nature of Heavy Runner's camp. He tried to warn them, to speak of the safe conduct, but his pleas were met with a chilling resolve. Baker, it is said, declared, "That makes no difference, one band or another of them; they are all Piegans [Blackfeet] and we will attack them." The order was given, and the massacre began. From the ridges above, soldiers fired into the lodges where people still slept. Chief Heavy Runner, clutching his paper of safe passage, ran towards the attackers, his voice a desperate cry against the onslaught. He was shot down, the promise in his hand rendered meaningless. Joseph Cobell, a scout, later claimed the shot, a grim act perhaps intended to divert attention from his brother-in-law Mountain Chief's camp, a mere ten miles downstream. The soldiers then charged, their actions recounted with chilling detail. William Birth of Company K boasted of slicing open lodge coverings with butcher knives, shooting the unarmed within. "We killed some with axes," he stated, a stark admission of the brutality. Survivors' accounts paint a vivid, horrifying picture. Spear Woman, a child of six, awoke to barking dogs, saw Heavy Runner fall, and hid behind a bed backrest as soldiers moved through the lodges, killing. Buffalo Trail Woman witnessed lodges surrounded, their occupants shot, then blankets and robes plundered before the tipis were torn down and set ablaze, burning even the wounded. Long Time Calf, only eight, escaped through the freezing Marias River, carrying her infant niece to safety. Bear Head, captured while catching horses, watched in agony as his mother, Fair Singing Woman, and other family members perished in the burning lodges, their bodies consumed by the flames. He counted fifteen men, ninety women, and fifty children dead. The official count by Baker's men was 173, though scout Joe Kipp later placed the number at 217. Only one cavalryman died, another injured in a fall. The aftermath was a stark silence, broken only by the crackling of burning lodges and the cries of the few who survived. This tragic event, born of mistaken identity and a brutal order, remains a dark stain upon the history of the Montana frontier, a testament to the devastating impact of unchecked military power and the profound injustices inflicted upon Native American communities.
